constraints.analysis_summary: Round 1 provides no information because no guess was made. Therefore, we have no constraints on the secret. All colors remain possible, and no positions are locked or misplaced.

From Round 2 we learn: Exactly two of the five guessed colors (red, blue, green, yellow, white) are in the secret. One of them is at its exact guessed position, the other is elsewhere. The three remaining guessed colors are impossible. All other colors (black) are possible. Duplicates are allowed. No prior information from Round 1.

Combining rounds 2 and 3: Round 2 says exactly two of {red, blue, green, yellow, white} are in the secret. Round 3 says exactly three of {green, yellow, white, black, purple} are in the secret. This leads to two mutually exclusive cases: (A) Red and blue are absent, exactly two of {green, yellow, white} are present, and exactly one of {black, purple} is present. (B) Exactly one of {red, blue} is present, exactly one of {green, yellow, white} is present, and both black and purple are present. In both cases, the secret contains exactly 3 or 4 distinct colors respectively. Additionally, round 2 had exactly 1 correct position (so one of its two existing colors is locked at its guessed position), while round 3 had 0 correct positions (so all three existing colors are misplaced from their round 3 positions). No specific color is yet confirmed as present or impossible across both cases. The locked position from round 2 could be any of positions 0-4 depending on which colors exist. All positions remain in play.

Case B: Exactly one of {red, blue} present, exactly one of {green, yellow, white} present, and both black and purple present. In both cases, the locked position from Round 2/4 (one correct position) and the three misplaced colors from Round 3 further constrain possible assignments, but no specific color or position is uniquely determined yet.

reasoning_steps: Step 1: IDENTIFY EXISTING COLORS - The guess is empty, so no colors are present. Therefore, no colors from this guess can be in the secret. The correct_pegs count is 0, which is consistent with no colors guessed.
Step 2: IDENTIFY LOCKED POSITIONS - No positions were guessed, so there are no locked positions from this round.
Step 3: IDENTIFY MISPLACED COLORS - Since no colors were guessed and correct_pegs is 0, there are no misplaced colors.

Step 4: IDENTIFY IMPOSSIBLE COLORS - No colors were guessed, so no colors are proven impossible from this round. The zero feedback applies only to the empty set, which provides no information about any specific color.

Step 5: IDENTIFY UNKNOWNS - All 4 positions remain unknown. All 6 colors (red, green, blue, yellow, white, black) are still possible. The secret could contain any combination of colors with any number of duplicates.

Step 2: IDENTIFY LOCKED POSITIONS - With 1 correct position among the 2 existing colors, exactly one of the guessed positions holds its color correctly. That position is locked, but which one is unknown.

Step 3: IDENTIFY MISPLACED COLORS - The remaining existing color (the other of the two) is in the secret but not at its guessed position. So it is misplaced. Which color and its wrong positions are unknown.

Step 4: IDENTIFY IMPOSSIBLE COLORS - The other three colors in the guess (the ones not among the two existing colors) are definitely not in the secret. Since we don't know which three, we cannot list specific colors yet, but they form a subset of {red, blue, green, yellow, white}.

Step 5: IDENTIFY UNKNOWNS - The secret has 5 positions. We have accounted for 2 colors (one locked, one misplaced). The remaining 3 positions must be filled with colors from the pool: the two existing guessed colors (possibly repeated) and black (the only color not guessed). All positions are distinct but duplicates are allowed. The identities of the two existing colors and their locked/misplaced statuses remain unknown.
